content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 4, 1993

Thomas Biesek
South Pasadena Taxpayers Association
324 Hawthorne Street
South Pasadena, CA 91030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-185

Dear Mr. Biesek:

You have requested advice on behalf of the South Pasadena
Taxpayers Association (herein referred to as "SPTA") concerning
the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").1/

QUESTION

Will SPTA incur campaign reporting requirements if it raises
money to pay for mailers opposing the development of a benefit
assessment district and a fire suppression district?

CONCLUSION

Money raised or spent by SPTA to gather petition signatures
to protest the district assessments are not reportable until one
of the issues is placed on an election ballot.

FACTS

SPTA is an informal political watchdog group that monitors
local municipal issues. Currently, the organization is not
registered as a committee and does not expend or raise monies.
However, SPTA has filed as a committee in prior years. SPTA
wishes to start raising money for a mailer which would include a
"protest form" to defeat a proposed benefit assessment district.
If a certain percentage of property owners sign the protest
petitions, the district assessment tax would automatically be

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

A-93-185
Page 2


defeated.  According to our telephone conversation on May 17,
1993, if a certain percentage of property owners do not sign the
petitions, the tax becomes effective.  In either case, the issue
does not become a ballot measure before the voters.  In addition,
SPTA is opposed to the formation of a fire suppression district.
However, under the relevant property owner protest rules if a
certain number of property owners sign protest petitions, the City
Council must decide either to place the issue on an election
ballot or to terminate the tax proposal.

     Attached to your letter of April 28, 1993, was a newsletter
that SPTA was considering mailing to taxpayers.  The newsletter
asks the public to contact legislators and express support
for two legislative bills.  You stated in our telephone
conversation on May 17, 1993, that SPTA would not incur $5,000 in
expenditures for this publication or similar publications in a
calendar quarter.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Act defines a measure as:

          ...any constitutional amendment or other proposition which is
     submitted to a popular vote at an election by action of a
     legislative body, or which is submitted or is intended to be
     submitted to a popular vote at an election by initiative,
     referendum or recall procedure whether or not it qualifies
     for the ballot.

                                        Section 82043.

     SPTA is planning to raise and spend money to defeat proposals
for a benefit assessment district and a fire suppression district
through the use of protest petitions.  Because the petition
processes do not qualify either of the issues for the ballot, SPTA
has no campaign reporting obligations.  (In re Fontana (1976) 2
FPPC Opinions 25 and Herzig Advice Letter, No. A-88-060.)2/

     If the City Council places the fire suppression district
issue on an election ballot, all contributions received and
expenditures made for the purpose of influencing the votes for or
against the measure must be disclosed if SPTA receives or expends
$1,000 or more in a calendar year.  (Sections 82015, 82025, 84200
and 84200.5.)  In addition, if SPTA provides to any other


_________________
2/    Copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available
in many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials
and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission
at a cost of 10¢ per page.

A-93-185
Page 3

Committee, including a sponsored committee, anything of value,
such as research, mailing lists, or opinion poll results, which
was obtained prior to the time that the City Council places the
issue on the ballot, the fair market value of the items will be
deemed nonmonetary contributions to the committee. (Section 82015
and Carmody Advice Letter, No. A-87-191.)

An organization that makes payments of $5,000 or more in a
calendar quarter to influence state legislative action files a
lobbying disclosure statement (Form 645). (Section 86115.) A
payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other
persons to contact legislative officials concerning pending
legislation is considered a "payment to influence" legislative
action. (Section 82045(e).) According to the facts, SPTA has not
met the requisite $5,000 threshold for filing lobbying disclosure
statements.

Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have additional questions concerning this matter.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

by: Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

QUESTION: Will
SPTA incur campaign reporting requirements if it raises
money to pay for mailers opposing the development of a benefit
a.:,sessment district and a fire suppression district?

CONCLUSION: Money raised or spent by SPTA to gather petition signatures
to protest the district assessments are not reportable until one
of the issues is placed on an election ballot.
